---
title: "Garrett_Paper"
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_document: default
date: "2024-11-21"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

The third model that we decided to use for this project was an ARIMA model, which is a model that we have not discussed in class but that we thought would provide a promising opportunity to work with the time series data. ARIMA stands for Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average and it's main goal is to "use time series data to either better understand the data set or predict future trends" (Hayes). For us, this meant that an ARIMA model would hopefully be able to better handle the time series data that we were using and could then use its understanding of the data to make predictions if we set the parameters of the model to the correct values. It also meant that, since we had a consistent stream of sales data over multiple years for Corporación Favorita and could see trends, future sales would most likely be more correlated to past sales which is exactly what ARIMA models are used for. ARIMA models are also extremely valuable when it comes to autocorrelation, which is "the degree of correlation of the same variables between two successive time intervals" (Taylor). Essentially, this means that ARIMA models can use a lagged value of a variable and compare it to the original value to optimally find patterns in the data that are related to specific time periods. This was especially valuable when it came to this data set because we needed to identify whether weekly or yearly trends (or both) impacted the overall sales data. The structure of an ARIMA model comes down to the actual code itself and the different parameters that we included within the model to attempt to better capture the relationships in the data. In our model specifically, it can be seen that there are 3 integers next to the ARIMA residual plots in the form [5,1,1]. In this case the "Arima" function was taken from the "forecast" package, and 5, 1, and 1 are the main parameters of the model. The 5 specifies the the number of past observations that are used to predict the current value it is tracking and is called the autoregressive term. The first 1 is used to make sure the time series is "differenced" (previous value subtracted from current value.) once so that the data then becomes stationary. It is extremely important for time series data to be stationary because this ensures that "mean, variance, and autocorrelation — remain unwavering over time. . . [Arima] models rely on the constancy of statistical properties like mean and variance over time. Non-stationary data can lead to unreliable model outputs and inaccurate predictions, just because the models aren’t expecting it" (Tate). For this specific model, we found that just one differencing of the data was enough to create a solid stationsary base for the model to predict on. The second 1 in the model is the number of lagged forecast errors that are used to predict. With a value of one, this means that the model uses the most recent error to adjust its prediction, and a value of one also makes sure that overfitting is avoided. With this lagged forecast error set to 1 the model can better understand patterns outside of the scope of the first two parameters and can better predict values that are not accounted for by differencing or the autoregressive term. These components are the base of the Arima model in general, but for our specific model


Hayes, Adam. “Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) Prediction Model.” Investopedia, Investopedia, www.investopedia.com/terms/a/autoregressive-integrated-moving-average-arima.asp. Accessed 20 Nov. 2024.

Taylor, Sebastian. “Autocorrelation.” Corporate Finance Institute, 21 Nov. 2023, corporatefinanceinstitute.com/resources/data-science/autocorrelation/. 

Tate, Andrew. “Understanding the Importance of Stationarity in Time Series.” Hex, 1 Dec. 2023, hex.tech/blog/stationarity-in-time-series/.
